question: What is diffusion?
answer: the movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration
accept: particles spreading from high to low concentration
particles moving from where there are many to where there are few
misconceptions: it is when particles are pushed in one direction
it only happens in gases
it is when particles get bigger

question: When a deodorant is sprayed, why does the person closest to the can smell it first?
answer: the particles are at their highest concentration near the can
they spread out (diffuse) into areas of lower concentration over time, so reach the nearest person first
accept: particles take time to spread to people further away
the nearest person is reached by the spreading particles soonest
misconceptions: the can pushes the particles towards the nearest person
the particles only travel in one direction
heavier particles fall near the can

question: Describe how the particles in a gas move.
answer: randomly, in every direction
accept: randomly in all directions
they move about randomly
misconceptions: they move in straight lines outwards from the source
they stay still until pushed
they move in one direction only

question: At the start, all the deodorant particles are clustered together near the can. Why do we call this an area of high concentration?
answer: there are many particles packed into a small area
compared with the surrounding space which has few particles (low concentration)
accept: lots of particles in one small region
the particles are crowded together in that area
misconceptions: high concentration means the particles are bigger
high concentration means the particles move faster
concentration refers to the smell strength only

question: Explain, in terms of particles, what happens over time after a gas is released into a room.
answer: the particles are moving randomly and are at first clustered together
they spread out from the area of high concentration to areas of low concentration
this continues until the concentration is even throughout the space
accept: particles move randomly and become evenly spread
they diffuse until concentration is the same everywhere
misconceptions: the particles all settle at the bottom of the room
the particles stop moving once spread out
something pushes the particles to spread

question: A student says the deodorant can pushes the particles out into the room. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: there is no mechanism inside the can to push the particles out
the particles spread out by themselves because they move randomly from high to low concentration
accept: nothing in the can propels the particles
they diffuse on their own, they are not pushed
misconceptions: the pressure inside the can drives them across the whole room
the can sprays particles in a fixed direction forever
the particles repel each other away from the can

question: Diffusion happens in gases. In what other state of matter does diffusion also occur?
answer: liquids
accept: in liquids too
liquid state
misconceptions: only in gases
only in solids
in solids and gases but not liquids

question: Diffusion of a gas continues until what point is reached?
answer: until the concentration is even (the same) throughout the space
accept: until evenly spread
until concentration is equal everywhere
misconceptions: until all particles return to the can
until the particles stop moving
until they reach the walls of the room

question: A drop of food colouring is added to still water and slowly colours the whole beaker without stirring. Name the process and explain why it happens.
answer: the process is diffusion
the colouring particles move randomly from high concentration to low concentration until evenly spread
accept: diffusion; particles spread from high to low concentration
it is diffusion because the particles spread out by themselves
misconceptions: it is filtration
the water pushes the colour around
the colour dissolves so it is not diffusion

question: A particle diagram of a gas is drawn with the particles in a neat, regular arrangement. Why is this incorrect?
answer: Gas particles should be randomly arranged, not in a regular pattern
options: Gas particles should be randomly arranged, not in a regular pattern
Gas particles should all be touching each other
Gas particles should be drawn much larger
Gas particles should be lined up along the bottom
distractors: touching particles describe a solid or liquid, not a gas
particle size has nothing to do with whether it is a gas
lining them up along the bottom is not how a gas behaves
misconceptions: a regular arrangement is fine for a gas
gas particles must be spread out already even before diffusion

question: Which statement best defines diffusion?
answer: Particles spreading out from where they are crowded to where they are sparse
options: Particles spreading out from where they are crowded to where they are sparse
Particles moving from a low to a high concentration
A change of state from a liquid to a gas
The trapping of large particles by filter paper
distractors: diffusion goes from high to low concentration, not the reverse
that describes evaporation, not diffusion
that describes filtration, not diffusion
misconceptions: diffusion moves particles from low to high concentration
diffusion is the same as evaporation

question: Why might a student wrongly draw the particles already spread evenly across the box when asked to show a gas just after it is released?
answer: They have assumed diffusion has already finished, instead of showing the starting point
options: They have assumed diffusion has already finished, instead of showing the starting point
Gas particles are always evenly spread at every moment
Even spreading shows the particles have stopped moving
Even spreading means the substance has become a solid
distractors: particles are only evenly spread after diffusion has happened, not at the start
even spreading does not mean particles stop; they keep moving randomly
an even spread does not turn a gas into a solid
misconceptions: particles are evenly spread at all times
an even spread means the particles are no longer moving

question: Describe how filtration separates an insoluble solid from a liquid.
answer: the mixture is poured through filter paper in a funnel
the larger solid particles are trapped as the residue while the liquid passes through as the filtrate
accept: the solid stays in the filter paper, the liquid runs through
insoluble solid caught as residue, liquid collected as filtrate
misconceptions: the funnel does the separating
filtration can separate dissolved substances too
the liquid is trapped and the solid passes through

question: What name is given to the liquid that passes through the filter paper during filtration?
answer: the filtrate
accept: filtrate
misconceptions: the residue
the solution
the solvent only

question: What name is given to the solid trapped in the filter paper during filtration?
answer: The residue
options: The residue
The filtrate
The solute
The solution
distractors: the filtrate is the liquid that passes through, not the trapped solid
the solute is a dissolved substance, which would pass through the paper
the solution is the whole mixture, not the trapped solid
misconceptions: the trapped solid is called the filtrate
the trapped solid is the solute

question: Explain why filtration cannot be used to separate salt from salt water.
answer: salt water is a solution in which both the solute and solvent particles are very small
both kinds of particle are small enough to pass through the holes in the filter paper, so nothing is trapped
accept: the salt particles fit through the filter paper just like the water
in a solution all particles pass through the holes
misconceptions: the salt dissolves so it no longer exists
filtration works but is just slow for salt
the funnel is the wrong shape

question: Why does sand get trapped by the filter paper while water passes through?
answer: the sand particles are too large to fit through the holes in the filter paper
the water particles are small enough to pass through the holes
accept: sand grains are bigger than the holes; water particles are smaller
the holes let small water particles through but not the large sand
misconceptions: the funnel filters out the sand
the water dissolves the sand
the filter paper attracts the water

question: Describe how evaporation is used to separate a dissolved solid from its solution.
answer: the solution is heated so the solvent evaporates (turns to gas)
the dissolved solid (solute) is left behind as crystals
accept: heat the solution to evaporate the solvent, leaving the solute as crystals
let the liquid evaporate so the solid stays behind
misconceptions: evaporation removes the solid and leaves the liquid
you boil away all the liquid completely
filtration would work just as well

question: When separating salt from salt water by evaporation, why should you not boil off all of the solvent?
answer: it can damage the evaporating basin or cause bits of the solute to escape (spit out) from the basin
accept: it could crack the basin or make the solid spit out
to protect the basin and avoid losing the solute
misconceptions: the salt would dissolve again
all the salt would evaporate too
it makes no difference

question: Because the solute is left behind as crystals, evaporation is sometimes given another name. What is it?
answer: crystallisation
accept: crystallization
misconceptions: distillation
filtration
condensation

question: Explain how distillation separates a mixture of two liquids that have different boiling points.
answer: the mixture is heated and the liquid with the lower boiling point boils and turns to a gas first
the gas passes into the condenser where flowing water cools it back into a liquid
the liquid drips into a beaker and is collected, while the other liquid stays behind in the flask
accept: lower boiling point liquid evaporates, is condensed and collected
heat to boil one liquid, cool it in the condenser, collect it
misconceptions: both liquids boil and are collected together
the liquid with the higher boiling point evaporates first
filtration could separate the two liquids

question: Chromatography separates dissolved substances such as the colours in ink. On what basis does it separate them?
answer: Their different solubilities
options: Their different solubilities
Their different boiling points
Their different particle sizes
Whether they are magnetic
distractors: different boiling points is the basis for distillation, not chromatography
filtration relies on particle size, not chromatography
magnetism is not used in chromatography
misconceptions: chromatography separates by boiling point
chromatography separates by particle size like a filter

question: In chromatography, why is the start line drawn in pencil rather than ink?
answer: pencil is insoluble in the solvent
so it does not dissolve and move up the paper with the solvent (which would ruin the result)
accept: pencil will not dissolve and travel up the paper
ink would dissolve and move, pencil stays put
misconceptions: pencil is easier to see
pencil dries faster than ink
it is just a convention with no reason

question: On a chromatogram, one ink sample splits into two separate colours as it rises up the paper. What does this tell you, and why does it happen?
answer: the sample was a mixture of more than one colour (not pure)
the more soluble colour travels further up the paper than the less soluble one, so they separate
accept: it is a mixture because it separated into two colours
the colours have different solubilities so move different distances
misconceptions: the sample was pure
the colour split because it boiled
the colour that moved least is the most soluble

question: On a chromatogram, a black ink sample does not move from the start line at all. What does this tell you about the black ink in that solvent?
answer: It is insoluble in the chosen solvent
options: It is insoluble in the chosen solvent
It is the most soluble sample
It is a pure single colour
It has the highest boiling point
distractors: the most soluble samples move furthest up the paper, not the least
not moving shows it is insoluble, it says nothing about purity
boiling point is not what makes a sample rise in chromatography
misconceptions: a sample that does not move is the most soluble
not moving means the ink is pure

question: A mixture contains sand and salt dissolved in water. Outline how you could separate all three components.
answer: filter the mixture to trap the insoluble sand as residue, collecting the salt water as filtrate
then evaporate the filtrate to leave the salt behind as crystals (the water can be collected by distillation)
accept: filter out the sand, then evaporate the water to get the salt
filtration for the sand, then evaporation for the salt
misconceptions: evaporate first then filter
filtration alone separates all three
you cannot separate this mixture

question: Ethanol has a boiling point of 78 degrees C and a melting point of -114 degrees C. During distillation the flask reaches 80 degrees C. How many degrees above ethanol's boiling point is this?
working: 80 °C − 78 °C = 2 °C, so ethanol has boiled and turned to gas
misconceptions: ethanol is still a liquid at 80 °C
the answer is 80 °C
you subtract the melting point

question: In a distillation, liquid A has a boiling point of 75 degrees C and liquid B has a boiling point of 100 degrees C. What is the difference in their boiling points?
misconceptions: the boiling points are the same
the difference is 175 °C
liquid B boils first

question: In the same volume of water, 5 g of solute A dissolves and 10 g of solute B dissolves. How much more of solute B dissolves than solute A?
answer: 5 g
working: 10 g − 5 g = 5 g more of solute B dissolves, so B is more soluble
misconceptions: A is more soluble
they are equally soluble
the difference is 15 g

question: Define a pure substance.
answer: a substance made of only one type of particle
accept: it contains only one kind of particle
misconceptions: a substance with nothing added
a substance that is clean or safe
a substance made of only one type of atom

question: Define a mixture.
answer: two or more substances that are not chemically bonded together
accept: two or more particles not chemically bonded together
misconceptions: substances that are stirred together
two substances bonded together
anything that is a liquid

question: What name is given to a substance that is not pure?
answer: impure
accept: an impure substance
a mixture
misconceptions: dirty
contaminated
dissolved

question: A box contains only carbon dioxide molecules. Each molecule is made of one carbon atom bonded to two oxygen atoms. Is this substance pure or impure, and why?
options: Pure, because it contains only one type of particle
Impure, because it contains more than one type of atom
Impure, because the particles are molecules not single atoms
Pure, because carbon dioxide is a gas
distractors: pupils wrongly judge purity by counting atom types inside a particle rather than the types of particle in the box
pupils think a particle must be a single atom to count as pure
being a gas is irrelevant to whether a substance is pure
answer: Pure, because it contains only one type of particle
misconceptions: a substance is impure if its particles contain more than one type of atom
a substance is pure only if every particle is a single simple atom
only gases can be pure

question: Explain why carbon dioxide is described as a pure substance even though its molecules contain different atoms.
answer: the box contains only one type of particle (carbon dioxide molecules)
the different atoms are chemically bonded together to form those molecules
accept: all the molecules in the sample are the same
the atoms are bonded into one kind of molecule
misconceptions: it is impure because it has two different atoms
pure means every atom must be identical
the word pure refers to a single particle not the whole substance

question: Which states of matter can a pure substance be?
options: Only a gas
Only a solid
Only a liquid
A solid, a liquid or a gas
distractors: pupils often think purity only applies to gases because gas examples are taught first
there is no reason a pure substance must be solid
there is no reason a pure substance must be liquid
answer: A solid, a liquid or a gas
misconceptions: only gases can be pure
only liquids can be pure
purity depends on the state of matter

question: A diagram shows a box containing two different types of particle that are not bonded together. Explain whether this substance is pure or impure.
answer: it is impure
because it is made of more than one type of particle
accept: impure / not pure
because there is more than one kind of particle present
misconceptions: it is pure because the particles are not bonded
it is a compound
you cannot tell from a diagram

question: What is a solute?
answer: the substance that dissolves into a solvent
accept: the substance that dissolves
misconceptions: the liquid that does the dissolving
the finished solution
a substance that does not dissolve

question: What is a solvent?
answer: the substance that does the dissolving, often water
accept: something that can dissolve another substance
misconceptions: the substance that dissolves
the crystals left behind
the solid that sinks to the bottom

question: What is formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent?
answer: a solution
accept: solution
misconceptions: a mixture only
a compound
a pure substance

question: Sugar is stirred into water until it can no longer be seen. What has happened to the sugar particles?
options: They have disappeared
They have spread out between the water particles
They have turned into water
They have been destroyed
distractors: the sugar looks like it disappears, but the particles are still there
dissolving does not change one substance into another
matter is not destroyed when something dissolves
answer: They have spread out between the water particles
misconceptions: the particles disappear when something dissolves
the solute turns into the solvent
the solute is destroyed

question: When sugar dissolves in water it looks as though it has disappeared. Explain what has really happened to the sugar.
answer: the sugar has not disappeared
its particles get pulled apart and become held between the water particles
accept: the particles spread out between the solvent particles
you just cannot see the particles, they are still there
misconceptions: the sugar disappears
the sugar melts into the water
the sugar particles are destroyed

question: What does it mean to say a substance is soluble?
answer: it is able to dissolve in a given solvent
accept: it will dissolve in a named solvent
misconceptions: it always dissolves in any liquid
it floats on water
it cannot dissolve

question: What does insoluble mean?
answer: it does not dissolve in a given solvent
accept: it will not dissolve in a named solvent
misconceptions: does not dissolve (without naming the solvent)
it dissolves slowly
it sinks but still dissolves

question: A pupil writes: 'Nail polish is insoluble.' Explain what is wrong with this statement.
answer: they have not named the solvent the nail polish is insoluble in
solubility depends on the solvent, so the solvent must be stated
accept: they did not say which solvent it is insoluble in
nail polish is insoluble in water but soluble in remover, so the solvent matters
misconceptions: nothing is wrong with the statement
nail polish never dissolves in anything
insoluble and soluble do not depend on the solvent

question: A student adds an unknown substance to water. It sinks to the bottom and stays there, even after stirring. Is it soluble or insoluble in water, and how can you tell?
answer: it is insoluble in water
because you can still see it / it has not dissolved
accept: insoluble, because it did not dissolve and is still visible
misconceptions: soluble, because it is in the water
insoluble (without explaining how you can tell)
it dissolved but the bits are heavy

question: Sodium hydroxide pellets are added to water and stirred. Eventually the pellets look as though they have disappeared. Is sodium hydroxide soluble or insoluble in water, and how can you tell?
answer: it is soluble in water
because the pellets dissolved / you can no longer see them
accept: soluble, because it dissolved and is no longer visible
misconceptions: insoluble, because the pellets are gone
soluble (without explaining how you can tell)
the pellets were destroyed

question: Nail polish can be washed off with remover but not with water. Explain why we say nail polish is insoluble in water but soluble in remover.
answer: a substance can dissolve in one solvent but not another
nail polish does not dissolve in water but does dissolve in remover, so you must name the solvent
accept: different substances are soluble in different solvents
it dissolves in remover but not water, so the solvent must be named
misconceptions: nail polish is just insoluble
solubility does not depend on the solvent
water dissolves everything

question: Salt is dissolved in water to make salt water. Which statement is correct?
options: The salt is the solvent and the water is the solute
The salt is the solute and the water is the solvent
The salt water is the solute
The salt is insoluble in water
distractors: the solute is what dissolves and the solvent does the dissolving, so this is reversed
the salt water is the whole solution, not the solute
salt clearly dissolves, so it is soluble in water
answer: The salt is the solute and the water is the solvent
misconceptions: the solvent is whatever was added second
the solution is the same as the solute
salt is insoluble in water

question: What is solubility?
answer: how easily a substance dissolves / the amount of solute that can dissolve
accept: how much of a substance can dissolve in a solvent
how easily something dissolves
misconceptions: whether something is a liquid
how fast something melts
the boiling point of a substance

question: In identical beakers of the same volume of water, 5 g of solute A dissolves and 10 g of solute B dissolves. Which solute is more soluble, and why?
answer: solute B is more soluble
because a greater mass can dissolve in the same volume of water
accept: B, because more of it dissolves in the same amount of water
misconceptions: A is more soluble because 5 g is enough
they are equally soluble
B is more soluble because it is heavier

question: Solute A dissolves to a maximum of 5 g and solute C dissolves to a maximum of 7 g in the same volume of water. What is the total mass of A and C that can dissolve?
misconceptions: subtracting to get 2 g
answering 7 g (the larger mass alone)
forgetting the unit g

question: A ball is placed in a beaker of water. Explain why this is described as a mixture and how the ball could be separated from the water.
answer: there are no bonds between the ball and the water
so they are not chemically bonded and form a mixture
the ball can be separated easily, for example with a net or a sieve
accept: the ball and water are not bonded, so it is a mixture
you can lift the ball out or sieve it out because nothing holds them together
misconceptions: it is a compound because the ball is in the water
they are bonded together
a mixture must always be two liquids

question: Explain why a solution, such as salt water, is described as a mixture.
answer: the particles of the solute and solvent are not chemically bonded together
no new substance has been made, so they can be separated again
accept: the solute and solvent are not bonded, so it is a mixture
nothing new is formed, so it is still a mixture
misconceptions: dissolving makes a new compound
the solute and solvent become chemically bonded
a solution is a pure substance

question: A box contains only carbon dioxide molecules. Why is this NOT a mixture, even though each molecule contains different atoms?
options: Because it is a molecule
Because the atoms are chemically bonded together into one type of particle
Because it is a gas
Because the atoms are not touching
distractors: simply saying 'because it is a molecule' does not explain why a molecule is not a mixture
being a gas has nothing to do with whether it is a mixture
whether atoms touch does not determine if something is a mixture
answer: Because the atoms are chemically bonded together into one type of particle
misconceptions: it is not a mixture just because it is a molecule
it is not a mixture just because it is a compound
gases cannot be mixtures

question: Describe what happens to the particles of a solute when it dissolves.
answer: they move apart and fit between the particles of the solvent
accept: the solute particles spread out among the solvent particles
misconceptions: they disappear
they sink to the bottom
they join chemically to the solvent

question: A pupil is asked why a particular diagram does NOT show a pure substance and writes only: 'because it should all be the same.' Why is this answer not good enough?
options: It corrects the diagram instead of explaining why the substance is impure
It is completely wrong science
It uses the wrong key word for impure
It does not mention the boiling point
distractors: the science is actually correct, so this is not the problem
the issue is not the key word used
boiling point is not relevant to identifying a pure substance
answer: It corrects the diagram instead of explaining why the substance is impure
misconceptions: correcting the mistake counts as explaining the error
the answer is wrong science
you must mention boiling point
